transcript: A Minnesota police officer's career has come to an end after this controversial high-speed pursuit.
St. Peter officer Thomas Winzel lost his peace officer license after the Minnesota Court of Appeals ruled his use of deadly force was unauthorized and excessive.
It was late September 16th, 2021 when police got a report of a burglary at Mill Pond Park.
Officer Winzel responded, finding two people near a camper.
While questioning one suspect, the other entered a red Hyundai that was parked nearby and sped off.
Officer Winzel pursued the suspect through residential streets and onto Highway 169, hitting speeds up to 133 miles per hour.
The fleeing suspect, later identified as 24-year-old Noah Gutham, crossed into the oncoming lanes of traffic and despite the escalating danger, officer Winzel continued.

It was at this point during the pursuit that Winzel's actions would become the focus of an investigation.

He struck the fleeing driver's vehicle multiple times with his squad car in an attempt to bring the chase to a stop in the southbound lanes of Highway 169, while Gutham's vehicle had its brake lights activated.
Under Minnesota law, deadly force is permitted only under specific circumstances, like protecting an officer or others from imminent death, or when apprehending a suspect who poses a similar threat.
The Minnesota Board of Peace Officer Standards and Training found that Winzel's actions during this pursuit did not meet those criteria.
The board's investigation found Winzel's use of deadly force was not only unnecessary, but it posed an extreme risk to public safety.
During the pursuit, Winzel struck the suspect's car four times and according to a board expert quote, the risk of danger was too great to continue the pursuit.
When Winzel was asked whether he considered terminating the pursuit, he said he intended to bring it to an end by striking the suspect's vehicle.
This incident led to disciplinary action against Winzel, and in 2023, following a thorough review and a hearing with an administrative law judge, the post board revoked his peace officer license.

The board said that the decision was necessary to protect the public from Winzel potentially using deadly force without authorization in the future, and that it provided a significant deterrent effect to other officers engaged in future high-speed pursuits.
According to Winzel's LinkedIn profile, he accepted a job as chief security officer for Tyson Foods in Kansas in September 2024.
Officer Winzel sustained an arm injury from his squad's airbag deployment and Noah Gutham was eventually convicted of gross misdemeanor criminal vehicular operation and felony fleeing.
A third charge of criminal damage to property was dismissed.
He got a stayed one-year prison sentence, three years of probation and credit for 52 days already served in jail.
Gutham was also ordered to complete the Minnesota adult teen challenge substance abuse program, which he was enrolled in at the time.
